hero.tagline: Get a working robot dev environment in an afternoon
hero.intro: Before you can do anything else, your laptop has to be able to talk to a robot - real or simulated. This branch is the install + first-bring-up checklist: ROS 2 (the operating system most robots run), Gazebo (a 3D simulator so you don't need hardware to start), MoveIt and Nav2 (the toolkits for arms and mobile robots), and the dev tools that keep you sane. Don't skim - the time you spend here is the time you don't waste later debugging a broken setup.

sections.heading: ROS 2 Jazzy on Ubuntu 24.04
sections.body: Jazzy Jalisco is the current LTS distribution (May 2024 - May 2029) paired with Ubuntu 24.04 (Noble Numbat). It's the default for new projects in 2026. Stick to LTS unless you have a specific reason - most upstream packages are tested against it first. If you're on Windows or macOS, run Ubuntu in a VM (UTM, VMware) or use WSL2 with caveats around real-time and USB.
bullets: ROS 2 distros are tied to Ubuntu LTS releases - Jazzy↔24.04, Humble↔22.04
Avoid mixing distros on one machine; use Docker if you need both
ros-jazzy-desktop ≈ everything most beginners need; ros-jazzy-ros-base for headless robots
On older hardware that needs Humble (Jetson Orin pre-JetPack 6, etc.), every command in this guide works with `humble` substituted for `jazzy`

codeBlocks.title: Add the ROS 2 apt repo
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y software-properties-common curl
sudo add-apt-repository universe
sudo curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ros/rosdistro/master/ros.key \
    -o /usr/share/keyrings/ros-archive-keyring.gpg
echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/ros-archive-keyring.gpg] \
    http://packages.ros.org/ros2/ubuntu $(. /etc/os-release && echo $UBUNTU_CODENAME) main" \
    | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ros2.list > /dev/null

codeBlocks.title: Install desktop + dev tools
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-desktop ros-dev-tools
codeBlocks.note: ros-jazzy-desktop pulls RViz2, demos, and the standard message packages. ros-dev-tools brings rosdep, colcon, vcs, etc.

codeBlocks.title: Source ROS in every shell
codeBlocks.code:
echo 'source /opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
echo $ROS_DISTRO   # → jazzy

codeBlocks.title: Smoke test - talker / listener in two terminals
codeBlocks.code:
# Terminal 1
ros2 run demo_nodes_cpp talker
# Terminal 2
ros2 run demo_nodes_py listener

sections.heading: Set up your colcon workspace
sections.body: colcon is the meta-build tool for ROS 2 packages. The convention is one workspace per project, with src/ for source, build/ install/ log/ generated by builds (don't commit these). Always source the install/setup.bash before running your nodes - it's the most common 'why doesn't this work' gotcha.

codeBlocks.title: Create and initialize
codeBlocks.code:
mkdir -p ~/{{ws}}/src
cd ~/{{ws}}
colcon build --symlink-install
echo "source ~/{{ws}}/install/setup.bash" >> ~/.bashrc
codeBlocks.note: --symlink-install lets you edit Python files without rebuilding.

codeBlocks.title: Resolve dependencies for everything in src/
Build only one package while iterating
Useful .gitignore for the workspace root
codeBlocks.code:
build/
install/
log/
.vscode/
*.pyc
__pycache__/

sections.heading: Gazebo simulator + ROS 2 bridge
sections.body: Modern Gazebo (formerly 'Ignition') ships separately from ROS and bridges to it. For Humble, Gazebo Fortress is the recommended pairing. Gazebo is great for sensor-rich, physics-correct simulations of mobile robots and arms; if you're doing GPU-heavy RL training, look at Isaac Sim or MuJoCo instead.

codeBlocks.title: Install Gazebo Fortress and the bridge
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-ros-gz \
    ros-jazzy-ros-gz-sim ros-jazzy-ros-gz-bridge \
    ros-jazzy-ros-gz-image

codeBlocks.title: Quick smoke test - empty world
codeBlocks.code: ros2 launch ros_gz_sim gz_sim.launch.py gz_args:='-v 4 empty.sdf'

codeBlocks.title: Bridge a ROS topic ↔ Gazebo topic
codeBlocks.code:
# /cmd_vel (ROS) ↔ /model/my_robot/cmd_vel (Gazebo)
ros2 run ros_gz_bridge parameter_bridge \
    /cmd_vel@geometry_msgs/msg/Twist@gz.msgs.Twist

sections.heading: NVIDIA Isaac Sim (for ML / GPU sim)
sections.body: Isaac Sim is the GPU-accelerated robot simulator from NVIDIA, built on Omniverse. It's the go-to environment for reinforcement learning, synthetic data generation, and photoreal sim-to-real workflows. Requires an RTX GPU.
bullets: Best for: parallel RL training (thousands of envs), photoreal vision, synthetic data
Skip for: classical mobile-robot dev (use Gazebo) or laptop without dGPU
Pairs with Isaac Lab (the policy training framework, formerly Orbit)

codeBlocks.title: Install via the Omniverse Launcher
codeBlocks.code:
# 1. Download Omniverse Launcher from https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/omniverse/download/
# 2. From the Launcher, install:
#    - Isaac Sim (latest)
#    - Cache, Nucleus (collaboration server)
# 3. Launch Isaac Sim - the first run downloads ~50 GB of assets

codeBlocks.title: Headless Python - useful for RL pipelines
codeBlocks.code:
# After install, isaac-sim ships its own python
~/.local/share/ov/pkg/isaac_sim-*/python.sh -c \
    "from omni.isaac.kit import SimulationApp; \
     app = SimulationApp({'headless': True}); print('ok'); app.close()"

sections.heading: MoveIt 2 (manipulation stack)
sections.body: MoveIt 2 is the canonical motion-planning framework. The fastest way to play is to install the binaries and launch the Panda demo. For your own arm, you'll generate a moveit_config package via the Setup Assistant from your URDF.

codeBlocks.title: Install via apt
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-moveit ros-jazzy-moveit-resources \
    ros-jazzy-moveit-visual-tools

codeBlocks.title: Build from source (when you need the latest)
codeBlocks.code:
cd ~/{{ws}}/src
git clone -b jazzy https://github.com/moveit/moveit2.git
vcs import < moveit2/moveit2.repos
cd ~/{{ws}}
rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y
colcon build --mixin release

codeBlocks.title: Launch the Panda demo
codeBlocks.code:
source ~/{{ws}}/install/setup.bash
ros2 launch moveit2_tutorials demo.launch.py rviz_tutorial:=true
codeBlocks.note: RViz opens with an interactive marker on the end-effector. Drag it, click Plan, click Execute.

codeBlocks.title: Run the Setup Assistant for your own URDF
codeBlocks.code: ros2 launch moveit_setup_assistant setup_assistant.launch.py

sections.heading: Nav2 (navigation stack)
sections.body: Nav2 is the standard for 2D mobile-robot navigation in ROS 2. The bringup package + TurtleBot 3 sim gets you to a working planner in minutes.

codeBlocks.title: Install Nav2 + sim
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-navigation2 ros-jazzy-nav2-bringup \
    ros-jazzy-turtlebot3-gazebo ros-jazzy-slam-toolbox

codeBlocks.title: Pick a TurtleBot model and launch
codeBlocks.code:
echo 'export TURTLEBOT3_MODEL=waffle' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
ros2 launch nav2_bringup tb3_simulation_launch.py headless:=False
codeBlocks.note: RViz opens. Click '2D Goal Pose', drop a target - robot plans + drives.

sections.heading: Visualization: RViz2 + Foxglove Studio
sections.body: RViz ships with ROS and is the workhorse for 3D + TF debugging. Foxglove is a more modern, layout-driven dashboard that shines for log replay and team sharing. Use both - RViz for quick on-robot debugging, Foxglove for deep dives into recorded bags.

codeBlocks.title: RViz2 (already installed with desktop)
codeBlocks.code: rviz2

codeBlocks.title: Foxglove Bridge - stream ROS 2 to the Foxglove app
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-foxglove-bridge
ros2 launch foxglove_bridge foxglove_bridge_launch.xml port:=8765
codeBlocks.note: Then open https://app.foxglove.dev and connect to ws://localhost:8765.

codeBlocks.title: Record a bag, replay later
codeBlocks.code:
ros2 bag record -o my_run /tf /scan /odom /image_raw
# later
ros2 bag play my_run

sections.heading: Docker dev environment
sections.body: Docker is the cleanest way to keep your host machine clean and to share an exact dev environment with collaborators. The official osrf/ros images are kept in sync with each release. On Windows / Mac, use VS Code's Dev Containers extension to get a full ROS 2 environment without the GUI hassles.

codeBlocks.title: Pull the official Humble desktop image
codeBlocks.code: docker pull osrf/ros:jazzy-desktop

codeBlocks.title: Dockerfile - your own ROS 2 dev image
codeBlocks.code:
FROM osrf/ros:jazzy-desktop

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    python3-colcon-common-extensions \
    python3-rosdep python3-vcstool \
    git nano && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Persistent workspace mount point
WORKDIR /root/{{ws}}
SHELL ["/bin/bash", "-lc"]
RUN echo 'source /opt/ros/jazzy/setup.bash' >> ~/.bashrc

CMD ["/bin/bash"]

codeBlocks.title: Run with X forwarding (Linux host)
codeBlocks.code:
xhost +local:docker
docker run -it --rm \
    --net=host \
    --env DISPLAY=$DISPLAY \
    --volume /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix \
    --volume $HOME/{{ws}}:/root/{{ws}} \
    my-ros-dev

sections.heading: vcs + rosdep - multi-repo workflow
sections.body: Most non-trivial robotics projects pull in several upstream repos. vcstool clones them all from a manifest, rosdep installs the system deps for everything in src/.

codeBlocks.title: my_project.repos - pin every upstream repo by branch or hash
codeBlocks.code:
repositories:
  navigation2:
    type: git
    url: https://github.com/ros-planning/navigation2.git
    version: jazzy
  slam_toolbox:
    type: git
    url: https://github.com/SteveMacenski/slam_toolbox.git
    version: jazzy

codeBlocks.title: Clone everything + install deps + build
codeBlocks.code:
cd ~/{{ws}}/src
vcs import < my_project.repos
cd ~/{{ws}}
rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y
colcon build --symlink-install

codeBlocks.title: Update all cloned repos to latest of their pinned branch
codeBlocks.code:
cd ~/{{ws}}/src
vcs pull

sections.heading: Dev workflow - VS Code, Cursor, Dev Containers
sections.body: Most ROS 2 development happens in VS Code (or Cursor - VS Code with Claude/Copilot integration). The plugin ecosystem covers ROS 2 specifically, plus C++/Python intellisense, Docker integration, remote SSH for working on a robot's onboard computer, and graphical debugging via launch.json. Dev Containers let you commit a ready-to-use environment so a teammate clones the repo and gets an identical setup with one click.
bullets: Install the official ROS extension - gives you launch file editing, package generation, msg/srv/action highlighting
C/C++ Microsoft extension - IntelliSense for C++, with .clangd for accurate cross-file analysis
Python + Pylance - Python type-checking, refactoring
Remote SSH - edit code that lives on the robot, with full IDE features
Dev Containers - one-click reproducible env on any host, including the robot
Use `print` debugging less - set breakpoints in launch.json with the C++ or Python debugger

codeBlocks.title: .vscode/c_cpp_properties.json - IntelliSense for ROS 2 includes
.vscode/launch.json - debug a Python ROS 2 node
.devcontainer/devcontainer.json - clone-and-run env

sections.heading: Real-time Linux (PREEMPT_RT) for control
sections.body: If your robot does any real-time control loop (force control, low-level joint torques, balancing), the stock Ubuntu kernel won't give you tight enough latency guarantees. PREEMPT_RT patches the kernel for predictable scheduling.
bullets: Stock Linux: ~1ms scheduling jitter, occasional spikes to tens of ms
PREEMPT_RT: ~50µs jitter, p99 in the 100–200µs range
Tradeoff: slightly lower throughput, occasional driver issues

codeBlocks.title: Install Ubuntu Pro real-time kernel (easiest path)
codeBlocks.code:
sudo pro attach <your-pro-token>
sudo pro enable realtime-kernel
uname -a   # → ...PREEMPT_RT...
codeBlocks.note: Free for personal use. Alternative: build the kernel yourself from kernel.org with the rt patchset.

sections.heading: Which ROS 2 distro should I pick?
sections.body:
ROS 2 LTS releases ship every two years and pair with Ubuntu LTS. Non-LTS releases ship between them - these get a year of support and are useful for testing what's coming, but most production projects stay on LTS. As of April 2026 the live picture is: Jazzy Jalisco (May 2024, EOL May 2029) is the default LTS for new work and the recommended choice for Isaac Sim 5.1 / 6.0; Humble Hawksbill (May 2022, EOL May 2027) is still everywhere in production and gets one more year of patches; Kilted Kaiju (May 2025) is the current non-LTS for previewing the next LTS but lacks official Isaac Sim support. The next LTS (Lyrical Lemur) is expected May 2026.

If you are starting a new project today, pick Jazzy. If you are pulling in Isaac Lab / Isaac Sim, Jazzy is the only LTS path that's officially tested. If your stack has Humble-only dependencies (some industrial drivers, older MoveIt 2 plugins), stay on Humble until May 2027 and migrate then.
bullets: Jazzy - Ubuntu 24.04, current LTS, default for any new project; works with Isaac Sim 5.1 / 6.0 + Isaac Lab
Humble - Ubuntu 22.04, EOL May 2027; pick only if your dependency stack hasn't migrated yet
Kilted - non-LTS, Ubuntu 24.04, ~1-year window; experimental Isaac Sim support only
Lyrical Lemur (next LTS) - expected May 2026 on Ubuntu 24.04 / 26.04
Mixing distros on one machine is painful - use Docker if you need both
rosdistro.ros.org has support windows, package coverage matrix, and EOL dates
Migration: Humble to Jazzy is mostly transparent; check the Jazzy migration guide for breaking changes around message types, lifecycle nodes, and rclcpp APIs

sections.heading: ROS 2 launch system, parameters, lifecycle
sections.body: Launch files start your nodes; parameters configure them; lifecycle nodes give you start / stop / cleanup hooks. Get fluent in all three or your stack will be impossible to operate. ROS 2 launch is Python-based (XML and YAML are also supported but Python is the most flexible). Parameters can be loaded from YAML, set on the command line, or queried at runtime via ros2 param.
bullets: launch.py: import LaunchDescription, Node, IncludeLaunchDescription; compose declaratively
Parameters: declare in code, override via --ros-args -p key:=value or YAML files
Lifecycle: configure → activate → deactivate → cleanup → shutdown - survival is up to you
Composition: ComponentContainer hosts many "components" in one process for IPC speed
Use ros2 launch --show-args to discover what a launch file accepts

codeBlocks.title: launch/my_robot.launch.py - start two nodes with shared params
codeBlocks.code:
from launch import LaunchDescription
from launch_ros.actions import Node
import os
from ament_index_python.packages import get_package_share_directory

def generate_launch_description():
    params = os.path.join(get_package_share_directory('my_robot'), 'config', 'robot.yaml')
    return LaunchDescription([
        Node(package='my_robot', executable='sensor_node',
             parameters=[params], output='screen'),
        Node(package='my_robot', executable='planner_node',
             parameters=[params, {'rate': 20.0}], output='screen')
    ])

codeBlocks.title: Override a parameter at launch time
codeBlocks.code:
ros2 launch my_robot my_robot.launch.py rate:=50.0
ros2 param list /sensor_node
ros2 param get /sensor_node use_filtered

sections.heading: Cross-compile for ARM (Jetson, Pi)
sections.body: Native compilation on a Jetson takes forever. Cross-compile from your x86 dev machine: build the package against an ARM sysroot, then rsync the install tree onto the robot. The fastest path is a Docker buildx multiplatform image; the canonical path is rosdep + colcon with --merge-install pointed at an ARM toolchain.

codeBlocks.title: Cross-build for arm64 with Docker buildx
codeBlocks.code:
docker buildx create --use --name multi-builder
docker buildx build \
    --platform linux/arm64 \
    -t my-registry/my-robot:arm64 \
    --load .
# Push to robot:
docker save my-registry/my-robot:arm64 | \
    ssh robot@jetson "docker load"

sections.heading: Learning paths - pick a starter project
sections.body:
11 branches is a lot to face cold. The trick to learning robotics is to commit to one concrete starter project and let it drag you through the relevant fields. Pick the path closest to what excites you. Each path below is structured so you can finish a working artifact in 1-3 weekends, and tells you exactly which branches to read in what order.

Don't try to read every branch front-to-back before building. The fields make sense once you've fought a real bug in your own code.
bullets: Path 1: Mobile robot from scratch (TurtleBot 3 sim → real)
Path 2: 6-DoF arm pick-and-place (Panda + MoveIt 2 in sim)
Path 3: Quadrotor in PX4 SITL (then build a tiny real one)
Path 4: Learn-from-demos manipulation (ALOHA / robomimic)
Path 5: Self-driving car in CARLA (perception → planner → control)

codeBlocks.title: Path 1 - Mobile robot (recommended starter for ROS 2 newcomers)
codeBlocks.code:
# Start: Setup → Navigation → Sensors → AMR → Compute (Jetson when ready)
# Why: low cost, full nav stack works in sim before you spend a dollar

sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-turtlebot3-gazebo ros-jazzy-nav2-bringup
export TURTLEBOT3_MODEL=waffle
ros2 launch nav2_bringup tb3_simulation_launch.py headless:=False
# Goal: drive between RViz-set goals in a known map → SLAM your own map →
#       deploy on a $300 TurtleBot 4 lite or Clearpath Husky

codeBlocks.title: Path 2 - Manipulator (best path if you like math)
codeBlocks.code:
# Start: Setup → Manipulation → AI/ML (skim) → Compute
# Why: kinematics + planning teach the math that powers everything else

sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-moveit ros-jazzy-moveit-resources
ros2 launch moveit2_tutorials demo.launch.py rviz_tutorial:=true
# Goal: pick a cube in MoveIt sim → write a Python pick-and-place loop →
#       try it on a real arm (UR3e or Franka via FCI)

codeBlocks.title: Path 3 - Quadrotor (most fun, least transferable)
codeBlocks.code:
# Start: Setup → Drones → Sensors → AI/ML (when adding vision)
# Why: you'll fly something within an hour

cd ~/{{ws}}/src && git clone --recursive https://github.com/PX4/PX4-Autopilot.git
cd PX4-Autopilot && make px4_sitl gz_x500
# Goal: takeoff/land via MAVLink → mission with waypoints → build a real <250g
#       drone (ELRS receiver + Pixhawk 6C + your own frame)

codeBlocks.title: Path 4 - Learn-from-demos (the modern frontier)
codeBlocks.code:
# Start: AI/ML → Manipulation (light) → Sensors
# Why: this is what serious manipulation labs ship in 2026

pip install robomimic gymnasium-robotics
python -c "from robomimic.utils import demo_collection_utils; print('ok')"
# Goal: collect 50 demos in sim with a teleop rig → train Diffusion Policy →
#       evaluate; iterate by adding more demos to the failure modes

codeBlocks.title: Path 5 - Self-driving in CARLA (most career upside)
codeBlocks.code:
# Start: Setup → Perception → Navigation → Autonomous-Vehicles → AI/ML
# Why: AV teams hire for this exact stack

# 1. Install CARLA 0.9.15+ from carla.org
./CarlaUE4.sh -RenderOffScreen
# 2. Add Autoware on top (full open-source AV stack)
docker run --rm -it --net=host --privileged \
    ghcr.io/autowarefoundation/autoware:latest-jazzy-runtime
# Goal: drive a known route in CARLA → swap the perception module for your own
#       YOLO-based detector → write a custom behavior planner

sections.heading: ROS 1 → ROS 2 migration cheat sheet
sections.body:
If you're coming from ROS 1 tutorials (Noetic, Melodic), almost everything you knew has a ROS 2 equivalent - but the names, build system, and idioms differ enough to trip you up. This section is the lookup table that saves you hours.

The big picture: roscore is gone (DDS handles discovery), CMake builds via colcon (not catkin), Python clients use rclpy (not rospy), C++ clients use rclcpp (not roscpp), launch files are Python (not XML by default), and message types live in their original packages but with new namespaces. Topics, services, and parameters still exist - the ergonomics changed, the concepts didn't.

Noetic (the last ROS 1 LTS) ends support in May 2025. If you're starting a new project in 2026, do not pick ROS 1.
bullets: roscore → gone. DDS does peer-to-peer discovery; just `ros2 run ...` and nodes find each other
rospy → rclpy. `import rospy` becomes `import rclpy` + class-based Node
roscpp → rclcpp. Class inherits `rclcpp::Node`; spinners use `rclcpp::spin(node)`
rosrun → ros2 run; rosnode → ros2 node; rostopic → ros2 topic; rosservice → ros2 service
catkin_make / catkin build → colcon build (`colcon build --symlink-install` is the daily command)
package.xml format 2 → format 3 (`<buildtool_depend>` etc. unchanged for most packages)
Launch files: XML still works, but Python is the modern default - imperative, composable
Message types: std_msgs/String → std_msgs/msg/String (the explicit msg/ subpath)
rosparam → ROS 2 parameters are per-node; declare with `declare_parameter(...)` then `get_parameter(...)`
Actions: actionlib → rclcpp_action / rclpy.action; goal handles + feedback streams largely the same
tf → tf2 (already in modern ROS 1 too); the API is identical in ROS 2
QoS: ROS 1 had no QoS profiles. ROS 2 requires matching them; mismatched profiles silently drop messages
Lifecycle nodes: a new concept (configure / activate / deactivate / cleanup), used by Nav2 + serious production stacks

codeBlocks.title: Minimal node: ROS 1 (rospy) vs ROS 2 (rclpy)
codeBlocks.code:
# ─── ROS 1 (rospy) ───────────────────────────────────────
import rospy
from std_msgs.msg import String

rospy.init_node('hello')
pub = rospy.Publisher('/chatter', String, queue_size=10)
rate = rospy.Rate(1)
while not rospy.is_shutdown():
    pub.publish('hi from ROS 1')
    rate.sleep()

# ─── ROS 2 (rclpy) ───────────────────────────────────────
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from std_msgs.msg import String

class Hello(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('hello')
        self.pub = self.create_publisher(String, '/chatter', 10)
        self.create_timer(1.0, self.tick)
    def tick(self):
        self.pub.publish(String(data='hi from ROS 2'))

rclpy.init()
rclpy.spin(Hello())

codeBlocks.title: Same task, different commands
codeBlocks.code:
# Build a workspace
catkin_make                 # ROS 1
colcon build --symlink-install   # ROS 2

# Run a node
rosrun my_pkg my_node       # ROS 1
ros2 run my_pkg my_node     # ROS 2

# List topics
rostopic list               # ROS 1
ros2 topic list             # ROS 2

# Bring up many nodes
roslaunch my_pkg demo.launch         # ROS 1
ros2 launch my_pkg demo.launch.py    # ROS 2

sections.heading: Your first week - what to actually do
sections.body:
Week-one for any of the paths above looks roughly the same. Don't try to optimize prematurely; just get the canonical demos running on your laptop, build muscle memory for the dev loop, then start customizing.

If any step takes more than half a day, you're probably stuck on environment / dependency issues - not on robotics. Check the Setup guides, the Dev workflow section, and the project's GitHub issues. Then come back.
bullets: Day 1: Install Ubuntu 24.04 + ROS 2 Jazzy (fresh VM is fine)
Day 2: Set up VS Code + colcon workspace + Docker; clone your path's starter repo
Day 3: Run the canonical demo (drive the TurtleBot, plan with MoveIt, etc.)
Day 4: Make one change you can see - different goal pose, different gripper, different wall in sim
Day 5: Read the relevant branch's first 3 sections; make one more change tied to a concept
Day 6: Bag a recording, replay in Foxglove, identify what surprised you
Day 7: Write down what you don't understand. Pick that as the focus for week 2
